Figure 8 — The Endpoint class receives and validates channel configuration before the Consumer runtime starts.

The Consumer Class: Where Kafka Meets CPI

The Consumer is the heart of the first public baseline.

In EventSmartKafka, the first release is Consumer-first.

This means the main validated flow is:

Kafka topic
     ↓
EventSmartKafka Consumer Adapter
     ↓
SAP Cloud Integration iFlow
     ↓
Downstream processing

The Consumer maps the Apache Camel ScheduledPollConsumer lifecycle to the Kafka KafkaConsumer polling model.

At runtime, the Consumer must:

  • initialize configuration
  • resolve credentials
  • connect to Kafka
  • subscribe to topics or assign partitions
  • poll records
  • optionally convert payloads
  • create a Camel Exchange
  • send the Exchange to the CPI pipeline
  • commit the Kafka offset only after successful processing
  • handle failures without silent data loss

The simplified runtime flow is:

KafkaConsumer.poll()
        ↓
ConsumerRecord<String, byte[]>
        ↓
raw passthrough or Avro conversion
        ↓
Camel Exchange
        ↓
Integration Flow pipeline
        ↓
successful processing
        ↓
commit offset

Reading from Kafka is not the hard part.

The hard part is committing the correct offset at the correct time.

 Consumer Class

Figure 9 — The Consumer maps the Camel polling lifecycle to Kafka record consumption.

Kafka to CPI Runtime Flow

Figure 10 — Consumer-first runtime flow from Kafka polling to SAP Cloud Integration message processing.

Why Offset Handling Is the Real Problem

A Kafka consumer can read a record in a few lines of code.

That is not adapter engineering.

Adapter engineering starts when something fails.

The key question is:

When is it safe to commit the offset?

For EventSmartKafka, the principle is simple:

Do not commit the offset until the message is successfully delivered to the CPI pipeline.

For raw passthrough, this means:

  • Read record
  • Create Exchange
  • Send payload to CPI pipeline
  • Pipeline succeeds
  • Commit offset

For Avro conversion, this means:

  1. Read record
  2. Validate payload
  3. Resolve Schema ID
  4. Fetch schema
  5. Decode Avro
  6. Create JSON or XML
  7. Send payload to CPI pipeline
  8. Pipeline succeeds
  9. Commit offset

If Avro conversion fails, the offset must not be committed.

Otherwise, the consumer group moves forward and the failed record is lost from the integration process.

That is exactly what a serious Kafka adapter must avoid.

The current behavior model is:

Scenario Offset Commit

Raw payload delivered successfullyYes
Avro converted and delivered successfullyYes
Missing Confluent Magic ByteNo
Invalid Schema IDNo
Schema Registry errorNo
Raw payload above hard limitNo
Conversion payload above hard limitNo
CPI pipeline failure with Stop on ErrorNo
CPI pipeline failure with Skip Failed MessageYes

This is why offset management belongs in the adapter layer.

If every iFlow must solve this independently, governance disappears.

Figure 11 — Offset commit must happen only after successful conversion and pipeline delivery.

Poison Pills and Silent Commits: The Two Failure Modes

A poison pill is a bad Kafka record that can be polled but cannot be processed. The consumer remains alive, but if the offset is not committed and no controlled strategy is applied, the same bad record is read again and again, blocking the next valid offsets.

A silent commit is the opposite problem. It happens when the adapter commits the offset even though the record failed. The consumer moves forward, the monitor may look green, but the failed message is lost.

EventSmartKafka is designed to make this behavior explicit: failed conversions are visible in CPI monitoring, poison messages are controlled by the selected error strategy, and offsets are committed only when processing is considered successful.

Figure 12 — Poison pills and silent commits are opposite failure modes: one blocks progress, the other hides data loss. A serious adapter must make both behaviors visible and controlled.

After iFlow Deployment: Runtime Lifecycle ASCII

[ SAP Integration Suite (CPI) ] Deploys iFlow
                             |
                             v
               Custom Adapter ESA is loaded
                             |
                             v
               [ Apache Camel ] Creates Component
                             |
                             v
           SdiaKafkaComponent.createEndpoint(...)
                             |
                             v
                 metadata.xml parameters
                     are mapped into
                SdiaKafkaEndpoint setters
                             |
                             v
           SdiaKafkaEndpoint.createConsumer(...)
                             |
                             v
               SdiaKafkaConsumer is created
                             |
                             v
                         doStart()
             ├── Validate Endpoint Configuration
             ├── Resolve CPI Secure Store Credentials
             ├── Build Native Kafka Client Properties
             ├── Instantiate KafkaConsumer Client
             └── Subscribe to Topic / Assign Partitions
                             |
                             v
             ScheduledPollConsumer Loop Starts
                             |
                             v
                         doPoll()
             ├── KafkaConsumer.poll() -> ConsumerRecord
             ├── Validate Payload Size Limits (Max 20MB)
             ├── Optional Conversion (AVRO/Protobuf -> JSON)
             ├── Create Camel Exchange & Inject Headers
             └── Dispatch Exchange to CPI Pipeline
                             |
                             v
                    CPI iFlow Processing
                             |
                +------------+------------+
                |                         |
         [ YES ]|                         |[ NO ]
                v                         v
          commitSync()             Evaluate Error Strategy
                |                   ├── 🔁 Retry Attempt
                v                   ├── ⏭️ Skip Message
         Next Poll Cycle            └── 🚨 Stop Pipeline

Runtime Class Responsibility

  • SdiaKafkaComponent: Creates the Endpoint and maps Camel parameters.
  • SdiaKafkaEndpoint: Stores and validates channel configuration.
  • SdiaKafkaConsumer: Starts Kafka connection, polls records, creates exchanges, controls offset commit.
  • SdiaKafkaAvroConverter: Converts Avro binary payloads into JSON/XML when conversion is enabled.
  • SdiaSchemaRegistryClient: Fetches and caches schemas by schema ID.

Compact Error Rendering

A custom adapter should not hide runtime failures behind generic Java stack traces.

For EventSmartKafka, conversion and payload validation errors are rendered in a compact CPI monitoring format.

The objective is to show the relevant runtime context directly in the monitor:

  • topic
  • partition
  • offset
  • bootstrap host
  •  schema mode
  • schema ID when available
  • error code
  • root cause

The current demo baseline includes visible error rendering for scenarios such as:

  • Avro payload without Confluent Magic Byte
  • invalid or unexpected Schema ID
  • Schema Registry lookup failure
  • payload above the configured conversion limit
  • payload above the raw passthrough limit

This matters because poison messages must be visible, traceable, and controlled.

A failed conversion should not silently commit the offset and disappear from the integration flow.

Figure 13 — Compact CPI error for Avro payload without valid Confluent Magic Byte.

Figure 14 — Compact CPI error for payload above the configured conversion limit.

What Has Been Tested So Far

The current Consumer demo baseline was validated in controlled non-production environments using trial, free-tier, or limited cloud resources.

Validated scope includes custom ESA deployment, iFlow channel creation, Kafka consumer startup, SASL/PLAIN over TLS, SCRAM-SHA-256/512, raw passthrough, Avro to JSON/XML, Schema Registry integration, Confluent Magic Wire, Fixed Schema ID, compact CPI monitor errors, conversion failure without offset commit, payload limits, and schema response limits.

This is not enterprise stress testing. It would be unrealistic to claim that an independent open-source project, developed and tested by one person, has validated every broker, security profile, network condition, payload size, partition strategy, retry behavior, and failure scenario.

All broker validations used trial, free-tier, or limited environments. These tests prove functional behavior under controlled conditions, not enterprise-scale certification.

Validated connectivity:
Confluent Cloud, Redpanda, Aiven.

Explored / partially tested:
IBM Event Streams.

What Is Explicitly Not in the First ESA

The first public ESA is Consumer-only.

It does not include validated Producer / Receiver role, Protobuf conversion, OAuth 2.0 production validation, full mTLS production validation, enterprise-scale performance certification, long-running endurance testing, vendor SLA, or SAP certification.

This is intentional. A community release should not mix validated functionality with roadmap items.

Figure 15 — EventSmartKafka - Custom Kafka.esa

What Part III Will Cover

Part III will explain how to create the custom adapter project from zero using SAP ADK and will provide the first downloadable .esa package on GitHub as a Community Demo / Experimental Technical Preview.

Users will be able to deploy the ESA in SAP Integration Suite, create a test iFlow, configure the EventSmartKafka Sender Adapter, and validate the Consumer runtime behavior.

The release will include basic documentation, configuration guidance, known limitations, and controlled test instructions.

The complete Java source code will be opened progressively in the next parts of the series.

Figure 16 — From blog to code to integration: Part III will provide the first downloadable ESA package on GitHub for controlled deployment and testing in SAP Cloud Integration.

Conclusion

Part I explained why EventSmartKafka exists.

Part II opened the runtime box and showed what happens after an iFlow is deployed: metadata.xml becomes UI, UI parameters become Endpoint properties, the Consumer starts, doStart() prepares the Kafka client, doPoll() reads records, and offset commit is controlled after processing.

The main lesson is simple: reading from Kafka is easy. Safe runtime behavior is the real adapter engineering challenge.

That means controlling poison pills, avoiding silent commits, exposing compact CPI errors, and committing offsets only when processing is considered successful.

EventSmartKafka remains a Community Demo / Experimental Technical Preview: not SAP-certified, not SLA-backed, and not a replacement for vendor-supported adapters.

Part III will explain how to create the SAP ADK adapter project from zero and will provide the first downloadable ESA package on GitHub for controlled testing.

The journey continues in Part III.

Part I - SAP BTP - CI - How I Built an Open-Source Enterprise Custom Kafka Adapter from Scratch - Part I

Ricardo Viana
Independent Solo Researcher

Creator of DEIP · SDIA · GDCR · DDCR · ODCP · EDCP · DDCP

#SAPIntegrationSuite #SAPCloudIntegration #Kafka #SAPADK #OpenSource #EventDrivenArchitecture #ApacheKafka #Avro #SchemaRegistry #CloudIntegration #SAPCommunity

entry.title: No Event Mesh? No Problem — Integrating Service Orders to FSM via CPI Polling
entry.content:
SAP Field Service Management (FSM) relies on timely and accurate service order data from SAP S/4HANA Cloud to dispatch technicians, schedule work, and close jobs efficiently. The standard integration between these two systems leverages SAP Event Mesh — a powerful, cloud-native messaging backbone on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) — to stream real-time service order lifecycle events into FSM.

However, not every customer has an active SAP Event Mesh subscription on BTP. This could be due to licensing constraints, cost considerations, or an IT strategy that minimises the number of BTP service subscriptions. For these customers, the standard event-driven integration path hits a wall before it even starts.

In this blog, I will walk through a practical workaround: a custom SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI) polling iFlow that periodically queries the S/4HANA Cloud Service Order OData API, formats the result as a synthetic Event Mesh payload, and hands it off to the standard "Replicate Service Order from SAP S/4HANA Cloud to SAP FSM" iFlow — no Event Mesh subscription required.

Note: This work will have some limitations and considersations before adopting.

How the Standard Integration Works

The standard SAP-delivered integration uses communication arrangement SAP_COM_0092 on the S/4HANA Cloud side to publish business events to SAP Event Mesh. A subscriber iFlow on CPI then picks up those events and replicates the affected service orders into FSM.

The four events subscribed to in the standard scenario are:

  • sap/s4/beh/serviceorder/v1/ServiceOrder/Released/v1
  • sap/s4/beh/serviceorder/v1/ServiceOrder/Completed/v1
  • sap/s4/beh/serviceorder/v1/ServiceOrder/ReleaseRevoked/v1
  • sap/s4/beh/serviceorder/v1/ServiceOrder/ChgdWhenReleased/v1

Each event carries a minimal payload (service order ID, UUID, type, and description). The CPI iFlow then fetches the full service order details and pushes them into FSM. This architecture is clean, near-real-time, and fully supported by SAP — but it has one hard prerequisite: SAP Event Mesh must be provisioned and configured on BTP.

The Challenge: Event Mesh as a Hard Prerequisite

For customers who have already established an S/4HANA Cloud-to-FSM integration but do not have an Event Mesh subscription, the above architecture creates a blocking dependency. Specifically:

  1. SAP Event Mesh is a paid, separately subscribed BTP service. Customers who have not planned for it may not have it available.
  2. Activating Event Mesh mid-project introduces procurement, provisioning, and configuration lead time — often weeks — that can delay go-live.
  3. Some customers deliberately keep their BTP footprint minimal. Adding Event Mesh solely for service order integration conflicts with their platform governance model.
  4. In brownfield or upgrade scenarios, re-configuring communication arrangements and queue subscriptions can introduce regression risk to existing integrations.

These constraints call for an alternative approach — one that achieves the same functional outcome (service order replication into FSM) without any dependency on SAP Event Mesh.

Workaround approach: CPI Polling iFlow

The workaround introduces a lightweight custom polling iFlow in CPI that mimics the role of SAP Event Mesh — without actually using it. The iFlow runs on a scheduled timer (for example, every 10 minutes), queries the S/4HANA Cloud Service Order OData API for records changed since the last run, constructs a synthetic Event Mesh-style JSON payload for each changed order, and invokes the standard "Replicate Service Order" iFlow via HTTPS — exactly as if the message had arrived from Event Mesh.

Crucially, the standard iFlow is left completely untouched. This ensures SAP support scope is preserved and future upgrades to the standard content remain non-breaking.

Layer

Component

Remark

Source

S/4HANA Cloud – API_SERVICE_ORDER_SRV OData API

Standard API, no change needed

Custom iFlow

CPI Polling iFlow (customer-built)

Scheduled every ~10 min

Payload Builder

Constructs synthetic EMS JSON message

Mimics Event Mesh envelope

Target

Standard CPI iFlow – Replicate Service Order

Called via HTTPS, unmodified

Destination

SAP FSM

Unchanged end target

 

Step 1: Querying Changed Service Orders

The polling iFlow calls the standard OData API below, substituting the timestamp from the last successful run (stored as a CPI local variable):

GET /sap/opu/odata/sap/API_SERVICE_ORDER_SRV/A_ServiceOrder
  ?$select=ServiceOrder,ServiceObjectType,ServiceOrderUUID,ServiceOrderDescription,ServiceOrderType,ServiceDocChangedDateTime,ServiceOrderIsReleased,ServiceOrderIsCompleted,ServiceOrderIsRejected
  &$filter=(
    ServiceDocChangedDateTime gt datetimeoffset'<LAST_RUN_TIMESTAMP>'
    and (ServiceOrderType eq 'SVO1' or ServiceOrderType eq 'SVO2'
         or ServiceOrderType eq 'RPO1' or ServiceOrderType eq 'RPO2')
    and (ServiceOrderIsReleased eq 'X'
         or ServiceOrderIsCompleted eq 'X'
         or ServiceOrderIsRejected eq 'X')
  )

The filter targets only the service order types that are relevant for FSM replication (SVO1, SVO2, RPO1, RPO2) and only records that are in a terminal or active business state (Released, Completed, or Rejected). This keeps the result set lean and avoids processing draft or in-progress orders that FSM does not yet need.

The last-run timestamp is read from a CPI local variable at the start of each execution and updated to the current timestamp at the end of a successful run. This creates a sliding window of changes and avoids re-processing already-replicated orders.

Step 2: Splitting and Iterating

The OData response may return multiple service orders in a single call. The iFlow uses a Splitter step to process each service order independently. This is important because:

  • A failure for one service order does not block replication of others.
  • Error handling and retry logic can be applied at the individual record level.
  • The standard iFlow expects a single service order event per invocation, matching the one-event-per-message model of SAP Event Mesh.

Step 3: Constructing the Synthetic Event Mesh Payload

For each service order, the iFlow builds a JSON payload that is structurally identical to what the standard iFlow would receive from SAP Event Mesh. The event type in the payload is determined by the status flags returned by the OData API:

  • ServiceOrderIsReleased = 'X'  →  event type: ServiceOrder.Released.v1
  • ServiceOrderIsCompleted = 'X'  →  event type: ServiceOrder.Completed.v1
  • ServiceOrderIsRejected = 'X'  →  event type: ServiceOrder.ReleaseRevoked.v1

A sample constructed payload looks like this:

{
  "type": "sap.s4.beh.serviceorder.v1.ServiceOrder.Released.v1",
  "specversion": "1.0",
  "source": "/default/sap.s4.beh/0LEUVB6",
  "id": "42010aef-4c3b-1fe1-8eaa-82de726150ac",
  "time": "2026-04-16T05:21:15Z",
  "datacontenttype": "application/json",
  "data": {
    "ServiceOrder": "8000000176",
    "CustMgmtObjectType": "BUS2000116",
    "ServiceOrderUUID": "42010aef-4c8d-1fd1-8de5-b5128633bb6f",
    "ServiceOrderDescription": "Annual maintenance visit",
    "ServiceOrderType": "SVO1"
  }
}

The id field can be populated using the ServiceOrderUUID, and the time field is set to the current execution timestamp. The source field should match the BTP source identifier configured in the target standard iFlow — check the iFlow's configuration parameters to confirm the correct value.

Step 4: Invoking the Standard iFlow via HTTPS

Rather than using a ProcessDirect channel (which would require modifying the standard iFlow to expose an internal endpoint), the custom iFlow calls the standard iFlow's public HTTPS endpoint. The technical user performing this call must be assigned the WorkspaceArtifactsDeploy role in CPI to have permission to trigger iFlow executions.

This design keeps a clean boundary: the standard iFlow remains a black box from the custom iFlow's perspective. If SAP updates the standard content in a future release, no changes are needed in the custom polling iFlow as long as the input message contract (the EMS JSON structure) remains stable.

Here is a crisp, highly structured version of your content, optimized for quick scanning and professional review.

⚠️ Limitations & Considerations: Polling Workaround

While this polling workaround is a pragmatic alternative, it lacks the native capabilities of an event-driven architecture. Review these 7 critical limitations before deploying to production.

1. Polling Latency (Near-Real-Time, Not Real-Time)

  • The Gap: Scheduled runs (e.g., every 10 mins) introduce a maximum data lag equal to the polling interval, whereas an event-driven model pushes changes in seconds.

  • Impact: Field techs may act on stale, modified, or canceled service order data on their mobile apps—a high risk for emergency work orders.

  • Mitigation: Shorten the polling interval during peak hours, balancing against S/4HANA Cloud API rate limits.

2. Snapshot-Only (No Intermediate States)

  • The Gap: Polling only captures a snapshot of the latest state. If an order quickly transitions through multiple statuses between runs (e.g., Released $\rightarrow$ Modified $\rightarrow$ Completed), intermediate states are lost.

  • Impact: FSM business rules triggered by specific state transitions (like ChgdWhenReleased or parts checks) will be skipped, causing workflow inconsistencies.

  • Mitigation: Redesign FSM business rules (if any) to trigger actions based on the final current state rather than historical transitions.

3. Missing ReleaseRevoked Context

  • The Gap: The standard integration publishes a distinct ReleaseRevoked event when a released order is recalled. Polling only detects a raw "Rejected/Revoked" status and cannot determine the historical sequence.

  • Impact: SLA timers or automations meant to cancel upon a revoke action may fail to trigger if the release and revoke both happen within a single polling window.

4. Increased S/4HANA Cloud API Load

  • The Gap: Unlike push events, polling repeatedly queries S/4HANA OData APIs on a fixed schedule, even when no data has changed.

  • Impact: Unnecessary network traffic and rapid consumption of your S/4HANA API license quotas.

  • Mitigation: Implement an adaptive schedule (e.g., poll less frequently overnight/weekends) and routinely monitor API usage logs.

5. Loss of ChgdWhenReleased Granularity

  • The Gap: The standard event model sends precise, field-level delta triggers (e.g., only the schedule time changed). Polling can only see that a change occurred, forcing a full-record refresh to FSM.

  • Impact: FSM automations that depend on specific field triggers (like sending an SMS to a tech only if the priority escalates) cannot be targeted cleanly. FSM will only see a bulk data overwrite.

6. Timestamp-Based Edge Cases

  • The Gap: Delta detection relies strictly on filtering the ServiceDocChangedDateTime field.

  • Impact: Clock drift between CPI and S/4HANA can cause skipped or double-processed records.

    If the CPI local variable storing the Last Run Timestamp is wiped (via redeployment or restart), data will be missed without a manual reset. Boundary records matching the exact timestamp filter might be dropped.

  • Mitigation: Build a 1–2 minute overlap window into your timestamp logic, and set up a monitoring alert for missing or stale timestamp variables.

Conclusion

The absence of SAP Event Mesh is not a roadblock for integrating S/4HANA Cloud service orders with SAP FSM. A custom CPI polling iFlow that mimics an Event Mesh payload offers a viable, non-invasive workaround. By keeping the standard "Replicate Service Order" iFlow completely unmodified, this conservative approach isolates the custom logic into a single, easily maintainable layer.

However, teams must accept the trade-offs: near-real-time latency, the loss of intermediate state history, and added operational complexity around error handling and timestamp management. With a robust retry mechanism and proper monitoring, this architecture can reliably support production workloads.

entry.title: Handling Large Volumes of Data Between SAP CPI and S/4 HANA
entry.content:
Most SAP CPI integrations work perfectly in development. Then you hit production — and everything changes.

500,000 sales orders. 2 million material records. Suddenly you're seeing timeouts, memory errors, and an S/4 HANA system that slows down for every other user. I've been there. Here's what actually works.

The Real Problem Is End-to-End, Not Just CPI

High-volume data processing touches four layers simultaneously — the ABAP database, the OData gateway, the Cloud Connector tunnel, and the CPI runtime. Most teams focus only on CPI and wonder why tuning iFlow settings doesn't fix anything. The answer is usually one layer below.

Layer 1 — Fix S/4 HANA First

Before writing a single iFlow step, go to your S/4 HANA system and check one thing: are your filter fields indexed?

When CPI filters OData results by LastChangeDateTime, the ABAP layer runs a SQL WHERE clause against the underlying table. Without a database index on that field, the database scans every single row — all 10 million of them — just to return 500 records.

That's the difference between a 0.3-second query and a 15-second one. Across 2,000 pages of data, that's 10 minutes versus 8 hours — while blocking ABAP work processes for every other user on the system.

Check indexes in SE11 → table name → Indexes tab. If your filter field isn't there, raise it with your BASIS team before anything else.

Also verify:

  • OData service is active in SICF and /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE
  • A dedicated technical user exists with the correct S_SERVICE role
  • Test the full OData call with $top=500 in /IWFND/GW_CLIENT before building

Layer 2 — Tune the Cloud Connector

The default connection pool in SAP Cloud Connector is 5 connections. For high-volume integration, that's a bottleneck you can't see — requests queue silently and your iFlow slows without throwing errors.

Increase the pool to 20–50 connections. Enable HTTP Keep-Alive. Enable GZIP compression on the system mapping — this reduces OData payload size by 60–70% and takes load off the tunnel significantly.

Layer 3 — Stop Fighting the OData Adapter

Here's the mistake I see most often: developers manually add $top=500&$skip=0 to the query string, then also enable Process in Pages in the OData adapter. The adapter adds its own pagination on top — values conflict, and you get duplicated pages or an infinite loop on page 1.

The CPI OData Receiver Adapter handles pagination natively. Enable Process in Pages, set your page size (200–500 records), and put only this in your query options:

  • $select — fetch only the fields you need (cuts payload by 60–80%)
  • $filter — for delta loading by date or key field

That's it. No $top. No $skip. Let the adapter do its job.

The Delta Load Pattern — Non-Negotiable at Scale

Never run full loads on recurring integrations with more than 10,000 records. Instead:

  1. Save the run timestamp to a CPI DataStore after each successful execution
  2. On the next run, filter with $filter=LastChangeDateTime gt datetime'<saved timestamp>'
  3. Process only what changed — update the timestamp on success

This turns millions of records into hundreds per run.

The Pattern That Holds at Production Scale

Scheduler

  → Read timestamp from DataStore

  → OData Adapter (Process in Pages ON)

  → Splitter (one record per message)

  → Process + write to target

  → Save new timestamp to DataStore

Simple. Resilient. Scalable.

Here is the skeletal working flow between S/4 OnPrem to CPI to pull data using Get.

  • Timer: OnDeployment
  • Content Modifier4:

Use timestamp variable to fetch data based on specific specific date

property timestamp to be given based on the abap field timestamp like(20000203062111)

 

 

  • Add looping process call
  1. Expression Type : Non XML
  2. Condition Expression: use ${property.Receiver1.OData.hasMoreRecords} is a CPI-internal exchange property that the OData Receiver Adapter sets automatically after each page call.
  3. Max. Numbers of Iterations: 10 (depends on the payload use this effectively, if you expect to receive 1lakh record use 100 to 120)
  4. Action When Max Iterations Reached: Throw Exception

 

 

 

  • add Request/Reply pallet to connect ODATA receiver adapter.
  • use ODATA V2 receiver Adapter

 

 

  1. For my testing using Page Size as 30
  2. Process in pages : checked

In the query there is no need of using $TOP and $SKIP, just make sure these are enabled in ABAP end that’s it,

CPI will take care in calling the looping process call until
Receiver1.OData.hasMoreRecords = ‘false’

 

 

 

 

  • Add ContentModifier2

Every new OData call replaces in.body completely. By the time the loop ends, you only have the last page — all previous pages are lost.

You are accumulating each page response into a property before the next OData call overwrites in.body.

Property name: appendPayload

SourceType: Expression
SourceValue: ${property.appendPayload}${in.body}

 

 

  • Add Content Modifier 3
  1. Under Main integration process add the property appendPayload to the Message body as ${property.appendPayload} to send it to target.
  2. Use all the transformational pallet functions under the Local Integration Process where ODATA is calling to S/4, so that pallet functions process data page wise, at the end you can append payload using the below approach.

 

Lets deploy and monitor

  • CPI Helper

 

  1. CamelLoopIndex : 3

is a built-in CPI property that tells you which iteration the loop is currently on.

  1. For the last looping index got Receiver1.OData.hasMoreRecords = false, next loop will end the process.
  2. SkipRecords_Receiver1.OData: is the property CPI uses internally to track and inject the $skip value into every OData page request — it increments automatically so you never need to calculate it yourself.

 

 

SkipRecords_Receiver1.OData

 

High-volume SAP CPI integration isn't complicated once you understand each layer's role. The teams that struggle are the ones treating it as a black box — tuning CPI while a missing database index silently kills performance.

Know your stack. Test each layer. Let the tools do what they're designed to do.

Have you faced high-volume integration challenges in your SAP landscape? Drop a comment — I'd love to hear your approach.

#SAP #SAPCPI #S4HANA #CloudIntegration #SAPIntegration #OData #CloudConnector #SAPDeveloper #IntegrationArchitecture

entry.title: SAP HANA Cloud Activation on SAP BTP and Database Operations Using JDBC from Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Introduction

While working on integration scenarios in SAP BTP, there are many cases where SAP Cloud Integration needs to directly read from or write to a database. SAP HANA Cloud is the obvious choice on BTP, and the JDBC adapter in Cloud Integration makes this integration straightforward—once the setup is clear.

In this blog, I will walk through:

  • How to activate SAP HANA Cloud in SAP BTP
  • How to create a simple database structure (schema, table, user)
  • How to connect SAP Cloud Integration to HANA Cloud using JDBC
  • How to perform basic SELECT, INSERT, and UPDATE operations from an iFlow

The focus of this blog is mainly on the enablement and configuration steps, because that is where most issues usually occur.

You can read my previous blogs here on SAP TMS and SAP ANS Integration with Cloud Integration

Content Transport Using SAP Cloud Transport Management Service for SAP CI CF Environment

Setting Up Alerts for SAP Cloud Integration Using SAP Alert Notification Service 

 

Prerequisites

Before starting, make sure you have:

  • Access to an SAP BTP Global Account
  • A Subaccount (Cloud Foundry environment)
  • SAP Integration Suite enabled
  • Required roles to:
    • Create HANA Cloud instances
    • Access HANA Database Explorer
    • Configure Cloud Integration

Step 1: Enable SAP HANA Cloud in SAP BTP

Check Entitlements

First, ensure SAP HANA Cloud is entitled:

  1. Go to BTP Cockpit ->Global Account ->Entitlements

Check if SAP HANA Cloud is available and assign quota to your subaccount if needed

If the entitlement is missing, you won’t be able to create a HANA Cloud instance.

Subscribe SAP HANA Cloud Tools application

  1. Navigate to Subaccount -> Instances and Subscriptions ->Create

Select Service: SAP HANA Cloud and Plan: tools

 

Create SAP HANA Cloud Instance

  1. Navigate to Subaccount ->Instances and Subscriptions->Create

Select Service: SAP HANA Cloud and Plan: hana
Provide Instance name (example: hana-cloud-ci-db) and System password

Create the instance , provisioning usually takes 20–30 minutes, so be patient here.

Verify Database Status

Once the instance is created:

Assign below roles to your user to open and perform operations in HANA Cloud 

Navigate to Subaccount ->Security->Users ->Assign Roles

  1. Open SAP HANA Cloud from the subaccount 
Make sure the database status is Running

Step 2: Create Database Objects

Open HANA Database Explorer

  1. From HANA Cloud Central->Open Database Explorer
Log in using the admin user (DBADMIN) 
\
Select SQL Console

Create Schema and Table

Create a schema first:

CREATE SCHEMA CI_DEMO_SCHEMA;

Now create a simple table:

CREATE TABLE CI_DEMO_SCHEMA.EMPLOYEE (

    EMP_ID INTEGER NOT NULL,

    EMP_NAME NVARCHAR(100),

    DEPARTMENT NVARCHAR(50),

    SALARY DECIMAL(15, 2),

    PRIMARY KEY (EMP_ID)

);

 

Insert some test data:

INSERT INTO CI_DEMO_SCHEMA.EMPLOYEE

VALUES (1, 'ABC DEF', 'IT', 90000);

 

Create a Technical User for Integration
Do not use DBADMIN for integration access.

CREATE USER CI_JDBC_USER PASSWORD "Pas****@1**3";
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE ON SCHEMA CI_DEMO_SCHEMA TO CI_JDBC_USER;

This user will be used later in Cloud Integration.

Step 3: Configure JDBC Connectivity in Cloud Integration

Get JDBC Connection Details

From HANA Cloud Central, note the host by Copy SQL Endpoint

98dd5c42*****************0d8792.hna1.prod-us10.hanacloud.ondemand.com:443

HANA Cloud JDBC URL format required  as per below :

jdbc:sap://<host>:443/?encrypt=true and Port 443 is used with SSL.

The complete URL will be become as per below :

jdbc:sap://98dd5c42*****************0d8792.hna1.prod-us10.hanacloud.ondemand.com:443/?encrypt=true

Navigate to Integration Suite->Security ->Manage JDBC Data Source and maintain as per below 

Configure JDBC Adapter in iFlow

HTTPS Sender to Trigger message from Postman and JDBC Receiver 

In JDBC connection maintain the JDBC Data Source name created in previous step.

Step 4: Testing

1. Select Operation 

Configure the Postman to trigger CI iflow to trigger the HTTP endpoint

SELECT * FROM CI_DEMO_SCHEMA.EMPLOYEE;
 

2. Insert Operation

<root>
    <insert_statement>
        <dbTableName action="INSERT">
            <table>CI_DEMO_SCHEMA.EMPLOYEE</table>
            <access>
                <EMP_ID>2</EMP_ID>
                <EMP_NAME>GGG HHHH</EMP_NAME>
                <DEPARTMENT>SAP</DEPARTMENT>
                <SALARY>99000.00</SALARY>
            </access>
        </dbTableName>
    </insert_statement>
</root>

 

Perform select again to view the inserted record 

3. Update operation :

<root>
    <update_statement>
        <dbTableName action="UPDATE">
            <table>CI_DEMO_SCHEMA.EMPLOYEE</table>
            <access>
                <EMP_NAME>NNN JJJ</EMP_NAME>
            </access>
            <key>
                <EMP_ID>2</EMP_ID>
            </key>
        </dbTableName>
    </update_statement>
</root>


Perform select again to view updated record 
Table from HABA Cloud DB 

Summary 

This POC highlighted several common pitfalls when working with SAP HANA Cloud and the JDBC adapter in SAP Cloud Integration. Most issues were not related to connectivity, but rather to user security policies, JDBC execution modes, and strict XML structure requirements. Understanding these early helps avoid hours of debugging.

Reference 

JDBC Receiver Adapter Payload Operations

SAP HANA Cloud

entry.title: The Challenge: Large Platform, Demanding Operations
entry.content:
SAP Integration Suite is a powerful platform for building and operating integrations at enterprise scale. Its design environment is feature-rich, its runtime is reliable, and its API coverage is broad. But for teams managing dozens — or hundreds — of integration flows across multiple environments, the operational side of the story can quickly become the bottleneck.

Consider a typical scenario: a new release is ready. The team needs to promote integration packages from development to test and then to production. Each package needs its externalized parameters re-applied for the target environment. Log levels need to be set appropriately. Each flow needs to be triggered for activation. If any object is locked, or if the deployment order is wrong, the team has to intervene manually. And when a flow is retired, there is no mechanism to transport that deletion — someone has to remove it by hand in each environment.

The same challenge applies to Partner Directory entries — a critical configuration object for many integration scenarios, especially pipeline-based architectures. Partner Directory entries have no built-in transport mechanism. Setting them up manually on a production system, under time pressure, with no rollback option, is a risk that teams simply should not have to accept.

None of this is a criticism of the platform. It simply reflects what happens when integration teams grow, release frequencies increase, and the margin for error shrinks. Manual processes that work fine for small teams become fragile at scale.

This is the problem that CICD Actions for SAP Integration Suite was designed to solve.

What Is It?

CICD Actions for SAP Integration Suite is an open-source CI/CD automation layer for SAP Integration Suite, built on GitHub Actions. It was developed jointly by SAP and a customer, born from real operational needs on a large-scale integration landscape. It has been running in production since October 2025 and is available today under the Apache 2.0 license:

https://github.com/SAP/cicd-actions-for-sap-integration-suite

A quick note on GitHub Actions — if you are not familiar with it: GitHub Actions is the built-in automation platform of GitHub. It lets you define workflows that run in response to events (a button click, a schedule, or a code push). No separate CI/CD tool to install. The entire deployment pipeline runs directly inside your GitHub repository.

The Core Idea: Git as the Single Source of Truth

The solution is built on one principle: Git holds everything — not just the integration flow source code, but also the configuration that goes with it: externalized parameter values per environment, deployment configuration per flow, Access Policies, and Partner Directory content.

GitHub Actions then uses this Git-stored configuration to fully automate the path from development to production. Deletions travel through the pipeline. Parameter changes are applied automatically. Log levels are managed after deployment. No manual intervention between environments.

SAP Integration Suite — DEV
     │  Download package as ZIP
     │  Extract + merge configuration
     ▼
Git Repository
     │  Change detection
     │  Environment-specific parameters stored as code
     ▼
SAP Integration Suite — TST / PRD / ...
     │  Upload to Design Time
     │  Activate to Runtime
     │  Apply environment-specific log levels
     ▼
Done — fully automated, fully auditable

Every step is recorded in Git. Every deployment is traceable. Every configuration change is reviewed before it is applied.

The Workflow Library at a Glance

The solution ships as a library of reusable GitHub Actions workflows and composite actions. Teams adopt it through a single sync workflow that pulls the latest templates automatically — when new features are released, your workflows update themselves.

 
Environment dropdowns are populated automatically from your configured GitHub Environments. DEV and TST are required with those exact names; any further stages — such as a pre-production or production environment — can be named freely.

Workflow What It Does

Download → Git → DeployDownload an Integration Package or Partner Directory from DEV, store it in Git, deploy to TST
Deploy / DeleteUpload or delete Integration Packages and Partner Directory entries to any configured environment
Delete from BTP and GitRemove an Integration Package or Partner Directory from all environments and from Git
Download to GitSnapshot Integration Packages or Partner Directory entries from any environment to a Git branch
Release ImportCalculate the delta between two Git refs and deploy changed packages to a target environment
Update iFlow ParametersApply externalized parameter changes to a specific iFlow and optionally redeploy
Access Policies (3 workflows)Download, upload, and manage Access Policies across environments
Dashboard GeneratorGenerate the operations dashboard
Create ReleaseVersioned release management from the development branch
 

Every workflow that operates on Integration Packages also supports Partner Directory entries — selected via a single mode input.

What's New in v1.3.0

Version 1.3.0 is the largest release to date.

The Dashboard — See Your Entire Landscape at a Glance

The headline feature is the Integration Suite Operations Dashboard: a static HTML page, automatically generated by a scheduled workflow and published to GitHub Pages. It shows every integration package and every IFlow across all configured environments — runtime status, deployed version, version drift between stages, and whether the package is managed by the pipeline. Color-coded health indicators make problem areas immediately visible.

The dashboard gives an immediate overview of every integration package across all environments — version drift, deployment errors, and unmanaged packages are visible at a glance.

Expanding a package reveals the full detail. Two typical situations a team encounters during release work:

Version drift — both IFlows are running a newer version on DEV and TST than on PRD. Typical during an active release: the new version has been promoted to test but not yet to production. Missing deployment — the second IFlow is configured for production but shows — in the PRD runtime column: it has not been imported yet. The deployment configuration says it should be there; BTP says it is not. Without this dashboard, that gap would likely go unnoticed until a runtime error surfaces.

The dashboard is regenerated daily and can be triggered manually at any time. Self-contained HTML — no server required.

GitHub App Authentication

v1.3.0 replaces Personal Access Tokens with GitHub App authentication. Two dedicated apps handle the read and write sides of the pipeline with minimal, explicit permissions. Token rotation is automatic.

Access Policies as Code

Access Policies can now be version-controlled and promoted through the pipeline. The pattern is identical to integration packages: download from DEV, commit to Git, review, deploy.

Automatic Log Level Management

The deployment pipeline now automatically sets iFlow log levels to an elevated setting before deployment and resets them to the configured operational level afterwards. No manual navigation to the operations cockpit after every release.

A Different Approach to Integration Lifecycle Management

 
SAP provides transport and lifecycle management capabilities for Integration Suite. This solution takes a different path: Git becomes the system of record, GitHub Actions drives the entire lifecycle, and every operation — download, deploy, delete, configure — is automated and auditable. For teams that have adopted a DevOps mindset and want full control over their integration pipeline, this is a deliberate alternative rather than an add-on.

Built Together, Open Sourced Together

This solution was developed collaboratively by SAP and a customer to reduce the manual overhead of release operations on a large enterprise integration landscape. It is available to any team, free of charge, under the Apache 2.0 license, and works on both GitHub Enterprise Cloud and GitHub Enterprise Server.

What's Coming in This Series

  1. The Download → Git → Deploy pipeline — what actually happens, step by step
  2. Managing parameters and log levels — environment-specific configuration as code
  3. The Release Import workflow — controlled promotion using Git tags and branches
  4. Access Policies as code — governance and auditability
  5. The Dashboard in depth — how to read it, configure it, and act on what it shows
  6. Extending the pipeline with Customer Exits — inject your own tests and deployment rules without touching the core

Repository and documentation: https://github.com/SAP/cicd-actions-for-sap-integration-suite

entry.title: Cloud Integration - Attachment names missing using the XI Sender Adapter
entry.content:
During the migration of interfaces from PI/PO to the Integration Suite, there were XI Protocol-based interfaces that sent attachments. The attachments were supposed to be saved as files on an SFTP server in the target system under their original names. The migration created an iFlow with an XI sender. Unfortunately, the original names of the attachments were lost. A solution has been implemented to save these names and use them when storing files on the SFTP server.

What's the problem?

An ABAP proxy can send not only the main payload but also attachments to the XI sender adapter. The attachments in the XI message are appended to the message by the sender adapter.

The following simple example scenario has been developed to illustrate this point. A message containing attachments is sent to Cloud Integration via an ABAP proxy. This message is received by a generic XI sender iFlow, which forwards it to an interface-specific iFlow via ProcessDirect, without losing the attachments or their original names. The specific iFlow should then store the main payload, along with all the attachments, under their original names on an SFTP server.

ABAP Proxy Message Monitor

The sample request shows that the attachments are Readme.txt and TestDocument.pdf. The received iFlow uses a Groovy script to log all the message's attachments, along with their names, in the MPL.

XI Sender iFlow - log attachments

def Message logAttachmentList(Message message) {
    // Log attachment infos with original names
    def attachments = message.getAttachments()
    if (attachments == null || attachments.isEmpty()) return message // No attachments
    def line = "----------------------------------------------------------\n"
    def attLog = ""
    def messageLog = messageLogFactory.getMessageLog(message)
    if (messageLog != null){
        attLog += "AttachmentCount: " + attachments?.size()?.toString() + "\n" + line
        attachments.eachWithIndex { att, index ->
            attLog += "Attachment: " + att?.key + "\n"
            def attachment = att?.value
            attLog += "ContentType: " + attachment?.getContentType() + "\n"
            attLog += "Name: " + attachment?.getName() + "\n" + line
        }
        messageLog.addAttachmentAsString("Attachmentlist.txt", attLog, "text/plain; charset=UTF-8")
    }
    return message
}

Unfortunately, the original names of the attachments are lost in the process, meaning they can no longer be read directly from the attachment object using the getName() method.

AttachmentCount: 2
----------------------------------------------------------
Attachment: payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460C8947@sap.com
ContentType: text/plain
Name: payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460C8947@sap.com
----------------------------------------------------------
Attachment: payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460CA947@sap.com
ContentType: application/pdf
Name: payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460CA947@sap.com
----------------------------------------------------------

How to solve the issue?

The message trace shows that the XI sender adapter stores the attachment reference as its name and the manifest content of the XI message in the SAP_XiManifest property. Both the reference and the original name are included here.

Property SAP_XiManifest

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" standalone="yes"?>
<Manifest wsu:Id="wsuid-manifest-5CABE13F5C59AB7FE10000000A1551F7" xmlns="http://sap.com/xi/XI/Message/30" xmlns:wsu="http://www.docs.oasis-open.org/wss/2004/01/oasis-200401-wss-wssecurity-utility-1.0.xsd" xmlns:ns2="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">
    <Payload ns2:href="cid:payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460C8947@sap.com">
        <Name>Readme.txt</Name>
        <Description/>
        <Type>ApplicationAttachment</Type>
    </Payload>
    <Payload ns2:href="cid:payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460CA947@sap.com">
        <Name>TestDocument.pdf</Name>
        <Description/>
        <Type>ApplicationAttachment</Type>
    </Payload>
    <Payload ns2:href="cid:payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460CC947@sap.com">
        <Name>MainDocument</Name>
        <Description/>
        <Type>Application</Type>
    </Payload>
</Manifest>


To solve this problem, a second Groovy script is added to preserve the original names. This script analyses the manifest and stores the references as key-value pairs in a map, using the reference as the key and the original name as the value. In order for the map to be passed as a header with the name XI_AttachmentList to the next iFlow, it is converted into JSON format.

XI Sender iFlow

def Message processData(Message message) {
    // Get SAP_XiManifest property
    def manifestXml = message.getProperty('SAP_XiManifest')
    if (manifestXml == null || manifestXml.trim().isEmpty()) {
        // No manifest found, return message as-is
        return message
    }
    
    // Parse the XML and create HashMap to store attachment names with their content IDs as keys
    def manifest = new XmlSlurper().parseText(manifestXml)
    def attachmentMap = new HashMap<String, String>()
    
    // Find all Payload elements where Type == "ApplicationAttachment",
    // Get the name and the href attribute and remove the "cid:" prefix
    manifest.Payload.each { payload ->
        if (payload.Type.text() == 'ApplicationAttachment') {
            def name = payload.Name.text()
            def attributes = payload.attributes()
            def hrefAttr = null
            attributes.each { key, value ->
                if (key.toString().contains('href')) {
                    hrefAttr = value
                }
            }
            
            if (hrefAttr && hrefAttr.startsWith('cid:')) {
                def contentId = hrefAttr.substring(4) // Remove "cid:" prefix
                attachmentMap[contentId] = name
            }
        }
    }
    
    // Convert HashMap to JSON string and store in a header
    message.setHeader('XI_AttachmentList', JsonOutput.toJson(attachmentMap))
    return message
}


Header XI_AttachmentList

Header XI_AttachmentList

{
    "payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460CA947@sap.com": "TestDocument.pdf",
    "payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460C8947@sap.com": "Readme.txt"
}

The second iFlow must allow the header XI_AttachmentList in the runtime configuration. As in the previous iFlow, all attachments are logged in the MPL along with their names using a Groovy script. However, the extended script takes into account the header variable XI_AttachmentList and maps the attachment name to its original name.

def Message logAttachmentListWithNames(Message message) {
    // Log attachment infos with original names
    def jsonAttList = message.getHeader('XI_AttachmentList', String)
    def attachmentNames = [:]
    if (jsonAttList != null) attachmentNames = new JsonSlurper()?.parseText(jsonAttList)
    def attachments = message.getAttachments()
    if (attachments == null || attachments.isEmpty()) attachments = message.getProperty('AttachmentsMap')
    if (attachments == null || attachments.isEmpty()) return message // No attachments
    def line = "----------------------------------------------------------\n"
    def attLog = ""
    def messageLog = messageLogFactory.getMessageLog(message)
    if (messageLog != null){
        attLog += "AttachmentCount: " + attachments?.size()?.toString() + "\n"
        attLog += "AttachmentNames:\n"
        attachmentNames.eachWithIndex { attNam, index ->
            attLog += attNam?.key + " = " + attNam?.value + "\n"
        }
        attLog += line
        attachments.eachWithIndex { att, index ->
            attLog += "Attachment: " + att?.key + "\n"
            def attachment = att?.value
            attLog += "ContentType: " + attachment?.getContentType() + "\n"
            attLog += "Name: " + attachment?.getName() + "\n"
            if (jsonAttList != null) attLog += "TranslatedName: " + (attachmentNames[att?.key]?:attachment?.getName()) + "\n" + line
        }
        messageLog.addAttachmentAsString("Attachmentlist.txt", attLog, "text/plain; charset=UTF-8")
    }
    return message
}

 

AttachmentCount: 2
AttachmentNames:
payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460CA947@sap.com = TestDocument.pdf
payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460C8947@sap.com = Readme.txt
----------------------------------------------------------
Attachment: payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460C8947@sap.com
ContentType: text/plain
Name: payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460C8947@sap.com
TranslatedName: Readme.txt
----------------------------------------------------------
Attachment: payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460CA947@sap.com
ContentType: application/pdf
Name: payload-5371C42CC5CD1FD192BE3937460CA947@sap.com
TranslatedName: TestDocument.pdf
----------------------------------------------------------

To simplify processing all the attachments, a Groovy script reads them into a property as a map. In addition, the JSON content from the XI_AttachmentList header is converted into a map and stored in the AttachmentNames property.

def Message saveAttachments(Message message) {
    // Store attachments in property
    def attachments = new HashMap<String, DataHandler>(message.getAttachments())
    message.setProperty('AttachmentsMap', attachments)
    // Store the number of attachments in property
    message.setProperty('AttachmentCount', attachments.size())
    // Clear the attachments
    message.getAttachments().clear()
    message.getAttachmentWrapperObjects().clear()
    // Get original attachment names and store as hashmap in property
    def jsonAttList = message.getHeader('XI_AttachmentList', String)
    def attachmentNames = [:]
    if (jsonAttList != null) attachmentNames = new JsonSlurper()?.parseText(jsonAttList)
    message.setProperty('AttachmentNames', attachmentNames)
    return message
}

All attachments are then processed using a Looping Process Call.

Receiver iFlow - Transfer attachments to an SFTP server

In this Local Integration Process, the current attachment in the loop is read and its contents are written to the message body. The AttachmentNames property is then used to determine the original file name, which is stored in the SFTP_FileName property. The Local Integration Process responsible for transferring the data to the SFTP server can then retrieve the file name from this property.

def Message getNextAttachment(Message message) {
    def attachments = message.getProperty('AttachmentsMap')
    if (attachments.isEmpty()) {
        message.setProperty('AttachmentCount', 0)
        return message
    }
    // Get next attachment to be processed
    def nextAttch = attachments.keySet().iterator().next()
    // Remove the attachment from the Map
    def attachment = attachments.remove(nextAttch)
    //def attachmentName = attachment?.getName()?:"attachment_"+attachments.size()
    def attachmentNames = message.getProperty('AttachmentNames')
    def attachmentName = attachmentNames[attachment?.getName()?:"attachment_"+attachments.size()]?:attachment?.getName()?:"attachment_"+attachments.size()
    
    // Replace the message with attachment
    message.setBody(attachment.getContent())
    // Update the AttachmentCount
    message.setProperty('AttachmentCount', attachments.size())
    message.setProperty('SFTP_FileName', attachmentName)
    return message
}

 

Conclusion

The XI Sender Adapter provides all the necessary information in the form of an XML manifest. Using this information alongside a short Groovy script enables us to easily save and reuse the original names of the attachments.

Useful links XI Sender Adapter:

  • https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-integration/sap-cloud-integration/configure-xi-sender-adapter 
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/cloud-integration-configuring-scenario-using-the-xi-sender-adapter/ba-p/13350089

entry.title: CICD Actions for SAP Integration Suite / The Download → Git → Deploy pipeline
entry.content:
The first post in this series introduced CICD Actions for SAP Integration Suite: what it is, why it exists, and what it looks like from the outside. This post goes inside the machine.

The Download → Git → Deploy workflow is the beating heart of the pipeline. It is the first workflow most teams run when they adopt the solution, and it is the one they use every time a developer changes something on DEV and wants to move it forward. Understanding exactly what happens in each step — and why those steps are designed the way they are — is the foundation for understanding everything else in the system.

  • The Problem It Solves
  • The Git Branch Model
  • What You Fill In
  • Inside the Pipeline
    • Authorization and Environment Resolution
    • Download and Commit to Git
    • Partner Directory in Git: Decoded and Readable
    • Clean Slate Before Deployment
    • Upload and Activate
  • What the Pipeline Does Not Do
  • Key Takeaways

The Problem It Solves

In a typical SAP Integration Suite landscape, a developer makes changes on the development tenant. Those changes live in the SAP Integration Suite design time. When it is time to promote them, someone has to manually export the package, import it into the test system, apply the right configuration values, and deploy each artifact — Message Mappings, Script Collections, Value Mappings, and iFlows. If anything was already deployed in test, it might need to be cleaned up first. Parameters might differ between systems. Log levels need attention. And none of it is recorded anywhere in a way that supports auditability or rollback.

The Download → Git → Deploy workflow replaces all of that with a single button press that is fully automated, fully recorded, and repeatable.

The Git Branch Model

Before walking through the pipeline, it helps to understand how Git is structured in this solution. There are two permanent branches with distinct roles.

The development branch is where all active work lives. Every package download, every configuration change, every new artifact lands here first. This is the branch the Download → Git → Deploy workflow writes to, and it is the branch the deployment jobs read from when uploading to TST.

There is no option to select a different target branch when triggering the workflow. The development branch is hardcoded as the destination. This is a deliberate design choice: developers should not have to think about Git branches at all during the day-to-day download and deploy cycle. The branch model is an infrastructure concern, not a developer concern. As landscape scenarios grow more complex — multiple parallel development streams, for example — this may evolve, but for the common case the simplicity is intentional.

The main branch represents what has been formally released. As long as work is progressing on development, main is untouched. The merge from development into main happens as part of the Release Import process — which creates a Git tag, a GitHub Release, and the clean reference point used for controlled promotion to production. That process is covered in a later post in this series.

What You Fill In

The workflow is (Dev) BTP Download to GIT and Upload/Deploy to TST, triggered manually via workflow_dispatch. The team member fills in three fields:

  • ID — the Integration Package ID or Partner Directory partner ID to process
  • Mode — IntegrationPackages or PartnerDirectory
  • JIRA Story / SNOW Incident — a reference that becomes the Git commit message, linking every change in the repository to a traceable work item

One important note on the ID field: the pipeline always operates on complete entities as its smallest unit. In IntegrationPackages mode, every operation applies to the entire Integration Package — there is no option to download or deploy a single iFlow or other individual artifact. In PartnerDirectory mode, the same principle applies: one complete Partner Directory entry per ID. This applies to all workflows in this CICD solution, not just this one.

All artifacts must be versioned — before triggering the workflow, every artifact in the package must have a saved version in SAP Integration Suite. Draft artifacts are not supported and will cause the download to fail. This is a standard practice in any version-controlled pipeline: only content that has been deliberately saved as a version is considered ready to leave the development tenant.

A note on Package IDs — if the ID in the screenshot looks unusual (KIhovby8X0TS8uXS3Oo1CCAcu), that is intentional. When creating a package in SAP Integration Suite, the ID is a free-text field you set once at creation time. Unlike the package name, it can never be changed afterwards. Using a GUID avoids any confusion down the line: names get renamed, reorganised, or reused — a GUID stays unambiguous forever.

Three fields, one button. The ID and Mode select what to process; the JIRA or SNOW reference becomes the Git commit message, tying every repository change to a traceable work item.

Inside the Pipeline

That is it for the inputs. Everything else is automated. The screenshot below shows a completed run for an integration package — 1m 34s from button press to a fully deployed package on TST. The actual runtime depends on the number of artifacts in the package: the deployment phase is typically the longest part of the process, as all artifacts are activated — Message Mappings, Script Collections, and Value Mappings are always deployed automatically, while iFlows are deployed based on their individual deployment configuration. The sections below explain what each phase does and why it is designed the way it is.

 

Authorization and Environment Resolution

Before anything touches SAP Integration Suite, the workflow verifies that the person who triggered it is a member of the authorized GitHub team. The team slug is defined in the BTP_DEV_TEAM_SLUG repository variable, configured once during the initial repository setup. If the check fails, the run stops immediately — no SAP Integration Suite credentials are used, no API calls are made. This applies to both modes.

Authorization is not just a security gate. It is also where the workflow binds to the DEV GitHub Environment — a named set of secrets and variables scoped to a specific job — and resolves all the connection details — BTP API URL, token endpoint, technical user, and service key identity — that every subsequent step will use. Before the deployment phase, the workflow binds to the TST GitHub Environment in the same way — resolving its own set of credentials and API endpoints. The team membership check is not repeated: it runs once, at the start of the workflow, and covers the entire run. No URLs or credentials are hardcoded anywhere in the workflow. Everything is environment-driven, which means the same workflow definition works across any landscape configuration without modification — adding a stage is a matter of adding a GitHub Environment with the appropriate credentials, not changing the workflow itself.

Download and Commit to Git

Once authorization is confirmed and the DEV environment is resolved, the pipeline checks out the development branch and removes the existing content for the package or Partner Directory ID. This clean-first approach ensures that if an artifact was deleted on DEV, it does not silently survive in the repository. The pipeline then authenticates with SAP Integration Suite, fetches the new content, and commits it — what lands in Git is an exact reflection of what is in SAP Integration Suite at that moment.

For IntegrationPackages mode, the package is fetched as a ZIP archive. The extraction goes two levels deep: the outer package ZIP is unpacked, and each artifact archive inside it is unpacked as well. The result is that real source files — iFlow configurations, Groovy scripts, Message Mappings, Value Mappings, Script Collections — land as individual readable files in Git. Every change to a Groovy script or a mapping is visible in the commit diff and reviewable in a pull request. It also opens the door for automated code scans — static analysis of Groovy scripts for security or quality requirements — as part of custom pipeline enhancements. And because the source files are plain text, the repository's built-in search becomes a lightweight where-used tool: searching across all packages for a specific endpoint URL, a function name, or a configuration value instantly shows every iFlow and script that references it.

For PartnerDirectory mode, the four object types are retrieved individually via API calls and written as four JSON files, one per type: String Parameters, Binary Parameters, Alternative Partners, and Authorized Users.

Regardless of mode, the commit uses the JIRA or SNOW reference entered at dispatch as the commit message. Every change in the repository is therefore traceable to a work item. Configuration and deployment control files for each iFlow are also created or updated as part of the commit — the detail on these is covered in the next post in this series. Crucially, these files are separate from the artifact content that is deleted and re-fetched on each download; any stage-specific parameter values or deployment settings the team has configured are preserved across repeated runs, not overwritten.

Partner Directory in Git: Decoded and Readable

The way Binary Parameters are stored in Git deserves a special mention. In the Partner Directory of SAP Integration Suite, Binary Parameter values are base64-encoded blobs — not human-readable in the portal. The pipeline stores them in two parts. The index file BinaryParameters.json lists each entry with its metadata and a filename reference instead of the raw encoded value. The actual content is decoded and written as a separate file under a Binary/ subfolder, named after the entry.

 

The Value field holds a filename reference, not a base64 blob. The decoded content lives as a separate file in the Binary/ subfolder.

The result is that an XSD schema used for Receiver Determination, or any other binary payload, is stored as its actual readable content in the repository. The example below is a simplified illustration — in practice this file would contain a full XSD definition.

 

The decoded file as it appears in Git. Every change to the content is visible in the commit diff and reviewable in a pull request — something simply not possible with an opaque encoded string.

Clean Slate Before Deployment

Before uploading the new version to TST, the pipeline clears what is already there. This happens in two layers, and the order matters.

For IntegrationPackages mode, the runtime artifacts are undeployed first. The order matters: if the design-time package were deleted while artifacts are still active in the runtime, a runtime restart would leave those artifacts unable to find their definitions — or worse, leave orphaned objects in the runtime that no longer have any design-time counterpart and that nobody remembers deploying. Cleaning the runtime first avoids that risk. Only once the runtime is clear is the design-time package deleted.

For PartnerDirectory mode, there is no runtime deployment to stop. The existing Partner Directory entries are deleted directly from the design time.

In both cases, clearing the design time completely before uploading is what makes the clean-slate guarantee possible. If an artifact was removed from the package on DEV, it will not survive on TST — there is no mechanism by which a deleted artifact can linger in the target environment. Without this step, TST would gradually accumulate ghost artifacts that no longer exist on DEV, making it an unreliable mirror of the source.

Upload and Activate

With the target tenant clean, the pipeline uploads the new content from the development Git branch and activates it.

For IntegrationPackages mode, the package source is reassembled into a ZIP — the format the SAP Integration Suite public API expects for package upload — and uploaded to the TST design time. The deployment engine then activates the artifacts in the package: Message Mappings, Script Collections, and Value Mappings are always deployed automatically. Integration Flows are deployed selectively — each iFlow has a deployment flag in its configuration file committed to Git, and only those marked for deployment in the target environment are activated. Since the package was deleted and re-uploaded, every deployed iFlow is new to the runtime and starts with the default log level of INFO. After deployment, the pipeline updates each iFlow's log level to whatever is configured for that environment in Git. No one needs to visit the operations cockpit to check or adjust anything afterward.

For PartnerDirectory mode, the pipeline creates the Partner Directory entries on the TST Cloud Integration runtime. Support for Edge Integration Cell runtimes is planned and will be added once the required public APIs become available.

What the Pipeline Does Not Do

The Download → Git → Deploy workflow intentionally stops at TST. It does not automatically promote to production. Moving to PRD — or to any further stage — is a deliberate, separate action. The Release Import workflow handles that step, using Git tags and branch comparisons to calculate exactly what has changed since the last release and deploy only the delta. That is the subject of a later post in this series.

Key Takeaways

  • The pipeline operates on complete entities as its smallest unit: one full Integration Package in IntegrationPackages mode, one complete Partner Directory entry in PartnerDirectory mode. Individual iFlows or artifacts cannot be targeted in isolation — this applies to all workflows in the solution.
  • A single workflow dispatch downloads a package from DEV, commits it to Git with a traceable reference, and deploys it to TST — fully automated, no manual handover.
  • The download creates and maintains one configuration file per package containing the parameter values for all iFlows in that package, and one deployment control file per iFlow — all committed alongside the package source in Git. Stage-specific values set by the team are preserved across repeated downloads. The detail is covered in the next post.
  • The clean-before-upload pattern ensures the target environment reflects Git exactly, including deletions.
  • Partner Directory is fully supported by the same pipeline. Switching the mode input is all it takes — the same authorization, Git commit, and deployment steps apply, with Partner Directory-specific handling where the content differs.
  • Every action that happens — every download, every parameter value, every deployment decision — is recorded in Git history.

The next post in this series covers the configuration layer in depth: how externalized parameters and log levels are managed per environment, how to add a new stage, and how to update a parameter value without triggering a full download.

Repository and documentation: https://github.com/SAP/cicd-actions-for-sap-integration-suite

entry.title: Run Pipeline for Cloud Integration on Edge Integration Cell with Partner Directory
entry.content:
1       Introduction

The goal of this blog is to explain how to run the Pipeline for Cloud Integration on your Edge Integration Cell for ground-to-ground integration of Pipeline scenarios in your private landscape.

The blog is intended for people who are familiar with Cloud Integration and Edge Integration Cell as well as with the Pipeline for Cloud Integration. In case you are new to Pipeline for Cloud Integration, make sure to check out this beginner’s blog to understand the idea behind it and to set it up initially.

As you know, the Pipeline heavily relies on the Partner Directory to dynamically influence the message processing behavior at runtime.

So far, the Partner Directory and its API was not supported on Edge Integration Cell. So, as an alternative to using the Partner Directory, @alex_bundschuh introduced an option to run the Pipeline for Cloud Integration without the Partner Directory, which was the first option in standard to use the Pipeline on Edge Integration Cell, but did not support all scenarios and features.

Since the latest release 2604 however (shipped in June 2026), the Partner Directory on Edge Integration Cell is available including the relevant APIs! With that, the whole Pipeline for Cloud Integration can run on Edge Integration Cell using the Partner Directory at runtime.

If you have an Edge Integration Cell connected to your Cloud Integration tenant, you will notice that in the Partner Directory UI you can switch between the Cloud Integration runtime and the Edge Integration Cell runtime. So, on each runtime you can define your partner IDs to support the Pipeline for Cloud Integration. Right now, it's not supported to copy over the partner IDs from one runtime to the other. This feature as well as an export/import of Partner Directory entries are planned to be shipped soon. As long as this is not supported, you can however use the Partner Directory API to synchronize the Partner Directory entries between the runtimes. Optionally, you can use the Partner Directory Accelerator tool which actually leverages the Partner Directory API.

Let’s take a look on how this works in detail.

2       Interaction with the Partner Directory on Edge Integration Cell

For the cloud runtime, there are two ways supported by SAP in standard to access the Partner Directory: The Partner Directory UI as part of Monitoring in SAP Integration Suite and the Partner Directory API as documented in SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

For Edge Integration Cell, the Partner Directory API can now be accessed! The API supports operations to create, read, update, and delete Partner Directory entries which are stored on Edge Integration Cell. For now, the requests to the Partner Directory API go via the cloud and therefore depend on cloud availability, not to be confused with the local OData API access in Edge Integration Cell.

The Partner Directory UI is a generic UI also supporting other use cases. To ease the creation and maintenance of configuration scenarios using the Pipeline for Cloud Integration, you can use an SAP open source project which has been specifically developed for the Pipeline for Cloud Integration. This accelerator tool relies on the Partner Directory API and works as well for Edge Integration Cell! Let’s explore these two options to interact with the Partner Directory on Edge Integration Cell.

 

2.1     How to use the Partner Directory API on Edge Integration Cell

To access the Partner Directory API on Edge Integration Cell, there are two steps to be done: 1) get a service key for the credentials to call the API and 2) find the location ID of your Edge Integration Cell to add it to the URL of the API request.

For 1), to get the service key, go to your BTP cockpit. From your subaccount, navigate to Services > Instances and Subscriptions > Instances. An instance of the service SAP Process Integration Runtime with plan api and the role AuthGroup_TenantPartnerDirectoryConfigurator needs to exist, with a service key containing all required credentials. For more information, please refer to the documentation or follow this guide on how to get your service key.

With this service key, you can access the API of your Partner Directory in the cloud. For that, take the URL property of your service key, add “/api/v1/”, and attach an endpoint of Partner Directory. For the Pipeline, there are currently 3 relevant endpoints, namely “AlternativePartners”, “BinaryParameters” and “StringParameters”, which you can find in SAP Business Accelerator Hub. With the credentials from your service key, you can use either OAuth or basic authentication, see the documentation for details. Now, you can send an HTTP request with the HTTP client of your choice (e.g. Postman or Bruno):

### call Partner Directory API in the cloud
GET {{url}}/api/v1/AlternativePartners
Authorization: credentials from your service key, either OAuth or basic authentication

To now access the same API of your Edge Integration Cell, you need its runtime location ID. You can find this runtime location ID on the Monitor page of your SAP Integration Suite tenant. For that, go to your tenant and select Monitor > Integrations and APIs. In the runtime dropdown menu, select your Edge Integration Cell and copy the runtime location ID from the URL shown in your browser:

The URL in your browser might look similar like this one:

https://{{tenantId}}.integrationsuite.cfapps.{{region}}.hana.ondemand.com/shell/monitoring/Overview/%7B%22edge%22%3A%7B%22runtimeLocationId%22%3A%{{runtimeLocationId}}%22%7D%7D

Extract the runtime location ID and add it to the following request:

### call Partner Directory API on Edge Integration Cell
GET {{url}}/location/{{runtimeLocationId}}/api/v1/AlternativePartners
Authorization: credentials from your service key, either OAuth or basic authentication

The result might be empty if you do not have any Partner Directory entries; the important part is that the API returns status code 200 as a response. With that, you have everything to set up the accelerator tool for your Edge Integration Cell API.

 

2.2     Setup Accelerator Tool using Partner Directory API of Edge Integration Cell

Once you know how to use the Partner Directory API of Edge Integration Cell via cloud, you got everything to setup and use the accelerator tool for Edge Integration Cell.

In case you are new to the tool, you can find it on GitHub as SAP open source project. As an introduction, there is a session available as part of Devtoberfest 2025 which shows the initial setup of the accelerator tool in detail to follow along. Here are several reasons why you should use the tool:

  • Overview of all Pipeline scenarios in the Partner Directory with advanced search option
  • XSLT generation with syntax check based on your tabular input
  • Support for string parameters as part of Point to Point scenarios and for message processing behavior
  • Easy switching between Partner Directory entries of tenants
  • Support for landscape stages with maintenance of SAP_Integration_Suite_Landscape
  • Replication of Partner Directory entries to another tenant of your landscape (e.g. from dev to test)
  • Migration of scenarios using tilde option for Pid
  • Merging of multiple XSLTs for receiver and interface determination

To use the accelerator tool with the Partner Directory API, all you need to do is to add a new tenant, as shown in this session. For Edge Integration Cell, make sure to add its runtime location ID in the URL input field, as explained in the earlier subchapter.

When saving your newly added tenant in the tool, you might not see any entries in case there are not yet any entries in your Partner Directory on Edge Integration Cell. As long as no errors pop up when saving your new tenant, the API calls work, and you can move on to the next chapter to create Partner Directory entries.

On a side note, so far, the tool was developed as an addition to the standard UI, so some actions might not be possible in the accelerator tool.
